content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 5, 1990


David Martin, Director
Government Affairs
Plumbing Manufacturers Institute
1655 N. Fort Meyer Drive, Suite 700
Arlington, VA 22209

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-89-717

Dear Mr. Martin:

     This is to confirm that your letter dated December 15, 1989
accurately reflects the advice provided to you by phone regarding
application of the campaign contributions provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("the Act").¹ I advised you that
corporations are permitted to give corporate funds to candidates
for state office in California, within the contribution limits of
the Act. I am enclosing a copy of the Act with sections
restricting campaign contributions marked.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Kathryn E. Donovan
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: Jonathan S. Rothman
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JSR:plh

Enclosures

_____________________________
     ¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Plumbing Manufacturers Institute
1655 N. Fort Meyer Drive, Suite 700
Arlington, VA 22209


December 15, 1989


Mr. Jonathan Rothman
Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Rothman:

Our trade association, the Plumbing Manufacturers Institute (PMI),
has been contacted by one of its corporate members to determine the
legality of giving company funds to candidates for state office in
California.

During our phone conversation on December 14, 1989, you advised me
that it was legally permissible for corporate money to be donated
to state candidates, if the contributions met the timing
requirements and amount limits set forth in California law.

The purpose of this letter is to request that the California Fair
Political Practices Commission provide PMI with a letter that
verifies that one of our member companies can legally give
corporate funds to candidates for state office in California.
Please address the letter to me, and have it signed by you, or the
appropriate legal official in your office. You may contact me at
(703) 522-2339, if there are any questions on this letter.

Thank you for your assistance on this issue of interest to our
membership.

Sincerely,

David F. Martin
Director, Government Affairs

cc: Richard W. Church

sections.conclusion: I advised you that corporations are permitted to give corporate funds to candidates for state office in California, within the contribution limits of the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Are corporations permitted to give corporate funds to candidates for state office in California, and if so, what are the applicable contribution limits and timing requirements?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Corporations are permitted to give corporate funds to candidates for state office in California, provided they comply with the contribution limits and timing requirements set forth in the Political Reform Act.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This document contains both the incoming request letter (December 15, 1989) from David Martin of PMI and the FPPC's response letter (January 5, 1990) from Kathryn E. Donovan, General Counsel. The FPPC response confirms informal telephone advice already provided. No formal Q/C sections with headers, but the questions and conclusions are clearly identifiable from the letters.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are corporations permitted to give corporate funds to candidates for state office in California, and if so, what are the applicable contribution limits and timing requirements?

CONCLUSION: I advised you that corporations are permitted to give corporate funds to candidates for state office in California, within the contribution limits of the Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirmed that corporations may contribute corporate funds to California state office candidates within the contribution limits of the Political Reform Act. The advice was originally provided by phone on December 14, 1989, and this letter confirms the guidance given.
